content.assessment: Consolidation with statutory mitigation is the proposal most directly framed around this lens's core criteria: it explicitly requires transport guarantees and equity compensation as conditions of closure, acknowledging that geographic and transport constraints are real barriers rather than administrative afterthoughts. Its reliance on zero-enrollment cliff cases as a rationale is well grounded in demographic evidence, but the model's resilience depends on resolving currently unaddressed rural transport frequency gaps, and consolidated destination schools risk generating new association-pressure cascades in neighboring villages not originally targeted, an effect directly documented in Hungarian precedent.
opportunities: Route-based multi-village transport logistics models used in minority-language school bus programs could inform the operational design of the required transport guarantees, though direct applicability to domestic consolidation is unconfirmed (F-live-school_network_planning-08)
strengths: Explicitly ties closure decisions to binding transport guarantees rather than leaving transport provision ambiguous, directly addressing a documented rural transport gap (F-live-school_network_planning-07)

Acknowledges zero-enrollment cliff cases as demographic facts requiring managed procedure rather than open-ended viability debate, aligning policy with documented population decline (F-live-school_network_planning-01)
threats: Consolidated destination schools may trigger 'association pressure' cascades affecting neighboring villages not originally targeted for closure, echoing the 2007 Nógrád county pattern (F-live-school_network_planning-05)

The causal link between consolidation and further depopulation remains contested across international evidence, meaning the network-resilience case for or against consolidation cannot be fully settled on current evidence (F-live-school_network_planning-06, F-live-school_network_planning-10)
weaknesses: Rural public transport frequency and scheduling gaps, especially in North-Eastern, South-Western, and Western regions, remain a documented practical barrier that statutory guarantees must overcome operationally, not merely declare (F-live-school_network_planning-07)
